notes.note: Quintilis was the old Roman name for the fifth month (counting from March), our July; it was later renamed Iulius in honour of Julius Caesar. Atticus had put off his arrival until then.

Quintus Cicero was canvassing for the aedileship (held 65 BC); Cicero urges Atticus to come in time for the vote (comitia). Aediles were elected magistrates in charge of public works, games, and the grain supply.
A protracted business dispute between Cicero and one Acutilius that Atticus had undertaken to settle on Cicero's behalf in Greece. Cicero repeatedly nags him to finish it in the early letters.
Intercessio here is Cicero's promised legal intervention or backing on Atticus's behalf, kept ready for use. (The same word also denoted a magistrate's formal veto.)

notes.anchor: have made an end of Gaius Macer
notes.note: As praetor presiding over the extortion court in 66 BC, Cicero secured the conviction of the historian and ex-tribune C. Licinius Macer. The condemnation of a popularis politician won Cicero great credit with the voters in his consular canvass.

notes.anchor: an ornament fit for my Academy
notes.note: Cicero named the gymnasium-and-garden at his Tusculan villa the 'Academy' after Plato's school at Athens, and stocked it with appropriate Greek statuary. The Hermathena was a fitting ornament for such a space.

notes.anchor: Minerva is the singular emblem of that gymnasium
notes.note: Hermes (Mercury) was the standard tutelary figure of any Greek gymnasium, so 'common to all,' whereas Athena/Minerva, goddess of wisdom, was the distinctive patron Cicero chose for his own scholarly retreat.

notes.anchor: they are at the Formian villa
notes.note: Cicero owned several country houses; here statues await transfer from his villa at Formiae (on the Campanian coast) to the Tusculan one near Rome. Adorning villas with Greek art was a mark of cultivated Roman wealth.

Marcus Licinius Crassus was the proverbially richest man in Rome; Cicero jokes that owning Atticus's library would make him richer still. Crassus would shortly form the so-called First Triumvirate with Pompey and Caesar.
